coredata.dc:title: Magneto-fluorescent superparamagnetic Fe3O4@SiO2@alginate/carbon quantum dots nanohybrid for drug delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Molaei, Mohammad Jafar
Salimi, Esmaeil
coredata.dc:description: Magneto-fluorescent nanohybrids are promising candidates for the application in drug delivery systems with simultaneous bioimaging. In this research, Fe3O4@SiO2 superparamagnetic nanoparticles were synthesized through the co-precipitation method to be used as a platform in drug delivery systems. The core/shell nanoparticles were coated with alginate (Fe3O4@SiO2@al). N-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were also synthesized by the hydrothermal method and were used in a nanohybrid with the alginate-coated Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles. The synthesized nanohybrid was used for drug loading and release experiments. Phase analysis, chemical bondings, magnetic properties, drug release characteristics, and particles’ size and morphology were studied by XRD, FTIR, VSM, UV–Vis, AFM, TEM, and FESEM techniques, respectively. TEM studies showed the successful synthesis of Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles with a mean particle size of around 20 nm. The Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs nanoparticles showed a saturation magnetization of 42.46 emu/g. The N-doped CQDs have fluorescence emission by excitation in the range of 210–400 nm. The synthesized nanohybrid exhibited 81% drug loading, and a 25% and 38% drug release within 48 h at a pH of 7.4 and 5.5, respectively. The higher rate of drug release in a simulated tumor environment compared to normal tissues makes multifunctional magneto-fluorescent Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs nanohybrid a promising candidate for simultaneous drug delivery and bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magneto-fluorescent
N-doped carbon quantum dots
Drug delivery system
Bioimaging
Magnetite

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The XRD pattern of the synthesized Fe3O4 nanoparticles, (b) UV–Vis spectroscopy of the CQDs, (c) PL emission spectra of the synthesized CQDs by excitation in the range of 210–400 nm, (d) CQDs solution under UV irradiation, (e) AFM image of the CQDs, (f) Particle size distribution histogram, and corresponding roughness data of the CQDs on the flat mica surface.
FTIR spectra of (a) CQDs, (b) DOX, (c) Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs hybrid, and (d) drug-loaded Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs hybrid.
(a) TEM images of the Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles, (b) The same sample at a higher magnification that shows a core/shell morphology.

(a) FESEM image of the synthesized Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs nanohybrid, (b) FESEM image of the synthesized sample with corresponding EDX chemical analysis of the present elements, (c) TEM image of the synthesized nanohybrid.

Magnetic hysteresis loops of the Fe3O4@SiO2 and Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs hybrid nanoparticles, (b) UV–Vis spectroscopy of the Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs hybrid, (c) PL spectra of the Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs hybrid nanoparticles by excitation at different wavelengths.
The DOX loading as a function of time for the Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs nanohybrid and (b) its release profiles at the pH values of 5.5 and 7.4 up to 144 h.

head.ce:title: Magneto-fluorescent superparamagnetic Fe3O4@SiO2@alginate/carbon quantum dots nanohybrid for drug delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shahrood University of Technology, Shahrood, 3619995161, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shahrood University of Technology, Shahrood, 3619995161, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magneto-fluorescent nanohybrids are promising candidates for the application in drug delivery systems with simultaneous bioimaging. In this research, Fe3O4@SiO2 superparamagnetic nanoparticles were synthesized through the co-precipitation method to be used as a platform in drug delivery systems. The core/shell nanoparticles were coated with alginate (Fe3O4@SiO2@al). N-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were also synthesized by the hydrothermal method and were used in a nanohybrid with the alginate-coated Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles. The synthesized nanohybrid was used for drug loading and release experiments. Phase analysis, chemical bondings, magnetic properties, drug release characteristics, and particles’ size and morphology were studied by XRD, FTIR, VSM, UV–Vis, AFM, TEM, and FESEM techniques, respectively. TEM studies showed the successful synthesis of Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles with a mean particle size of around 20 nm. The Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs nanoparticles showed a saturation magnetization of 42.46 emu/g. The N-doped CQDs have fluorescence emission by excitation in the range of 210–400 nm. The synthesized nanohybrid exhibited 81% drug loading, and a 25% and 38% drug release within 48 h at a pH of 7.4 and 5.5, respectively. The higher rate of drug release in a simulated tumor environment compared to normal tissues makes multifunctional magneto-fluorescent Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs nanohybrid a promising candidate for simultaneous drug delivery and bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Targeted drug delivery by the application of nanoparticles could deliver therapeutic molecules and drugs to the diseased site. Among the used nanoparticles, magnetite (Fe3O4) has been used extensively in drug delivery systems. Superparamagnetic magnetite could exhibit zero residual magnetization when the external applied magnetic field is removed. By applying an external magnetic field and microwave energy, Fe3O4 nanoparticles could absorb the external energy and convert it to heat resulting in an increment in the tumor temperature. The temperature increase could change the structure of the proteins and lipids and enhance the cell membrane's permeability [1]. Magnetite is a ferromagnetic material and can be transformed into a superparamagnetic one when the size of the particles is reduced and its magnetism is converted from multi-domain to single-domain. The size reduction in the single-domain regime results in a reduction in the coercivity value until it reaches zero and the nanoparticles exhibit superparamagnetic characteristics. Chemical co-precipitation is a promising synthesis method for the preparation of superparamagnetic iron oxides due to less hazardous chemicals which are required and ease of implementation. Generally, the superparamagnetic state of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles can be reached when the size reaches below 20 nm [2].

Multifunctional hybrid nanostructures with the ability of fluorescence imaging and drug delivery have attracted substantial attention in recent years. The magneto-fluorescent hybrid nano-carriers are potential platforms for delivery of the drugs with simultaneous in vivo and in vitro bioimaging of the drug accumulation in the target [3]. Most of the magneto-fluorescent nanomaterials need surface modifications to be soluble in water in cases they are synthesized by using organic solvents. Therefore, aqueous synthesis of these structures with water-solubility is desirable [4].

CQDs are dots that consist of carbon atoms and can be functionalized with biomolecules or organics. The size of CQDs is typically less than 10 nm, along with fluorescence emission ability [5]. CQDs have ease of synthesis and modification, chemical and photostability, water dispersibility, good cell permeability, low toxicity, and excitation-dependent fluorescence emission. Therefore, these types of dots are potential candidates for the application in bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery, and other biomedical applications [6].

It has been found that if the synthesized drug delivery systems contain nanoparticles with low colloidal stability, they can be cleared from the bloodstream before the realization of their targets. The lack of colloidal stability could be overcome by the application of stabilizers such as polymers to prepare stable colloids of the magnetic nanoparticles [7]. Alginate extracted from the marine bacteria and brown algae is an anionic hydrophilic copolymer which is consisted of alternating monomers of β-Dmannuronic acid (M units) and 1–4 linked α-l-guluronic acid (G units). Alginate is biodegradable, biocompatible, and mucoadhesive. Therefore alginate has found potential applications in different drug delivery systems [8]. The alginate can be degraded and eliminated from the body after releasing the drug due to its biodegradability [9].

Ma et al. [10] synthesized mesoporous Fe3O4/CQDs/silica microspheres and investigated their PL characteristics. However, the saturation magnetization of these microspheres was low. The synthesis of SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs/DOX showed both pH and heat-responsive drug release behavior [11]. Hyaluronic acid-modified porous carbon-coated Fe3O4 nanoparticles have also been synthesized and were used for photothermal/chemotherapy of tumors [12]. Magneto-fluorescent FeN@CQDs conjugated with riboflavin and folic acid were used in photodynamic therapy and photothermal therapy [13].

In this study, a magneto-fluorescent nanohybrid drug delivery system based on Fe3O4 superparamagnetic nanoparticles was developed to be used as a platform for loading anticancer drugs. The Fe3O4 nanoparticles were coated with a shell of SiO2 increasing their biocompatibility. Then, the Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles were covered with an alginate layer to act as a biocompatible coating as well as a platform for attaching CQDs. The alginate may also act as a reservoir for the drug and increase the drug loading efficiency. The magnetic properties, optical properties, drug loading, and release of the synthesized nanohybrid were investigated.

Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), iron (II) chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH), citric acid, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), isopropanol, and alginate were purchased from Merck. Branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals were laboratory grade and were used without further process.

The Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized through a co-precipitation process. Briefly, 0.6 g FeCl2·4H2O and 1.6 g FeCl3·6H2O were added to 50 ml of deionized water after grinding in a mortar. The solution of iron chlorides was stirred for 1 h to ensure complete dissolution of the chlorides in the water. The solution was transferred into a flask with three necks and was agitated vigorously under an inert gas atmosphere for 30 min. By dropwise addition of the 30 ml NH4OH to the solution, brown precipitates formed. After the complete addition of NH4OH, the flask was maintained in an oil bath at a temperature of 80 °C for 70 min. Then the precipitates were collected with an external magnet and were washed several times with deionized water to adjust the pH and then were dried in an oven overnight.

The dried Fe3O4 nanoparticles were dispersed in 30 ml citric acid solution (0.1 M) by using an ultrasonic bath for 1 h. Then the suspension was stirred vigorously for 12 h. The surface-modified nanoparticles were collected with an external magnet and washed with deionized water several times before drying in an oven under a vacuum.

40 mg of surface-modified Fe3O4 nanoparticles were dispersed in 20 ml isopropanol with the aid of an ultrasonic bath. Then, 5 ml of ammonium hydroxide and 600 μl of TEOS were added to the suspension under vigorous stirring. The reaction let to progress under stirring within 6 h. The core/shell nanoparticles were collected by a magnet and washed with deionized water and ethanol several times to remove individual silica precipitates. The particles were dried in an oven for further treatment.

The CQDs were synthesized using citric acid and PEI as the starting materials by the hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.2 g of citric acid was dissolved in 80 ml of deionized water and was stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, 0.002 g of PEI was added to the solution and the temperature was increased to 60 °C. The solution was stirred for 1 h and then was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave was placed in an oven at 180 °C for 3 h. The color of the solution converts from colorless to light brown after the hydrothermal treatment. The obtained solution was dialyzed by a dialysis tube against deionized water for 72 h to remove uncreated species. The water outside of the dialysis tube was refreshed every 12 h.

For the synthesis of surface-modified Fe3O4@SiO2@alginate nanocomposite (Fe3O4@SiO2@al), 10 mg of alginate was added to 30 ml of deionized water. The alginate solution was stirred for 1 h at 50 °C. Subsequently, 0.4 mg of surface-modified Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles was added to the alginate solution. The suspension was stirred vigorously for 5 h at room temperature. The alginate-treated nanoparticles were collected and washed with a 4000 rpm centrifuge to adjust pH. The nanoparticles were dried in an oven at 60 °C overnight.

To attach CQDs to the Fe3O4@SiO2@al nanoparticles, 10 ml of the CQDs solution was mixed with the formerly prepared nanoparticles. The suspension was stirred vigorously for 8 h. Then the nanoparticles were collected with the aid of a centrifuge and washed with deionized water several times to remove any un-attached CQDs from the suspension. The collected and washed nanoparticles (Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs) were dried in an oven at 60 °C.

To evaluate the drug loading and release characteristics of the synthesized nanocomposite, DOX was used as the model drug. For drug loading and release measurements, 0.8 g of the synthesized nanocomposite was added to 40 ml of PBS solution and stirred for different periods. Then 2 ml of DOX drug containing 2 mg/ml of DOX was added to the above suspension and stirred for 12 h in dark. The DOX-loaded nanocomposite was collected with the aid of a centrifuge and dried at 50 °C. To determine the loaded drug, the supernatant liquid was collected and its absorbance value was determined with UV–Vis spectroscopy. The drug loading was calculated according to the following equation:
Drug loading percent =
M a
−
M b
M a
× 100 %
where Ma was the amount of the drug in the initially added solution, and Mb was the amount of the drug in the supernatant liquid which were calculated based on the absorbance values of UV–Vis spectroscopy [14]. The UV–Vis measurements for calculating DOX concentration in different samples were recorded at 490 nm (as the characteristic peak of DOX [15]) using a calibration curve.

The release of the drug from the nanocomposite was performed at two different pHs of 5.5 and 7.4. For each of the investigated pH values, 0.4 g of the DOX-loaded nanocomposite was dispersed in a 20 ml PBS solution, and the whole suspension was transferred into a dialysis tube. The nanocomposite-containing dialysis tube was immersed in 50 ml PBS at different pHs of 5.5 and 7.4. The experiment continued far from light. In regular time intervals, an aliquot of the PBS outside the dialysis tube was removed for further analysis and an equal amount of fresh PBS was added to the container. The aliquot at different intervals was measured for absorption in UV–Vis spectroscopy. The amount of the drug in each sample was determined according to a standard curve. The DOX release percentage was calculated based on the following equation:
DOX release percentage =
V ×
C t
Z
× 100 %
where ct is the concentration of DOX at time t calculated from the absorption of UV–Vis spectroscopy, V is the volume of the sample, and Z is the total amount of DOX in t = 0 in the dialysis tube.

Phase analysis of the synthesized nanocomposite was performed on a Philips PW3710 XRD machine with a step size of 0.02° in 2 θ = 5–80° using Cu-Ka radiation. The mean crystallite size was calculated by the Williamson-Hall method based on the XRD peaks broadening [16].

The chemical bonding in the nanocomposite was studied with Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). A PerkinElmer FTIR spectrometer in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 was used. The size and morphology of the synthesized CQDs were studied by an atomic force microscope (AFM) of Park Scientific Instruments, CP, Auto probe. The preparation of the samples was conducted by using a drop of the CQDs-containing solution on a freshly cleaved mica surface after evaporation of the solvent. A Philips CM30 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) operating at 200 kV was applied for the morphological studies of the synthesized nanoparticles. For the sample preparation of TEM, the synthesized nanoparticles were dispersed in ethanol in an ultrasonic bath for 10 min. Then a drop of the suspension was brought on a carbon-supported copper grid. The nanoparticles were left on the grid after solvent evaporation. The absorption studies were conducted on a Shimadzu UV-1750 spectrophotometer. The magnetic properties were recorded by a Meghnatis Daghigh Kavir (Iran) magnetometer with the maximum applied field of 5 kOe at room temperature. The magnetization at the maximum applied field was considered the saturation magnetization.

Fig. 1 a shows the XRD pattern of the synthesized Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The XRD pattern confirms the formation of the Fe3O4 phase while other iron oxides are not formed. The XRD pattern exhibits the peaks corresponding to (002), (113), (004), (115), and (044) planes of the Fe3O4 phase. The mean crystallite size for the formed magnetite phase was calculated to be 18 nm.

UV–Vis spectroscopy of the synthesized CQDs is shown in Fig. 1b. The UV–Vis spectrum of the CQDs shows a peak at 245 nm that could be attributed to the π–π* transition of C = C bonds [17] and a peak at 365 nm that could be due to the oxygen-related surface states corresponding to n-π* transition of –CO bond [18].

Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of the synthesized CQDs are illustrated in Fig. 1c. The CQDs exhibit PL emission by excitation in the range of 210–400 nm. The PL spectra in Fig. 1c confirm that PL emission in CQDs is excitation wavelength-dependent which is a characteristic of the CQDs that have been synthesized through different routes [5]. It has been claimed that the excitation-dependent PL behavior of CQDs is due to the particle size distribution and surface state [19]. The fluorescence of the CQD solution can be realized by the naked eye from the observed emission under UV lamp irradiation in Fig. 1d. The size distribution of the CQDs can also be realized from the topography of the embedded dots on a flat surface obtained by AFM. The AFM image in Fig. 1e confirms that the mean particle size of the observed CQDs is below 10 nm. The size distribution histogram of the synthesized CQDs in Fig. 1f is consistent with the PL results for these dots. The CQDs can emit fluorescence emission when are small enough (preferably below 10 nm). When the carbon nanoparticles are larger, cannot show PL characteristics [6].

The FTIR spectra of CQDs, DOX, bared nanocomposite, and drug-loaded nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 2 . The CQDs show peaks in 1420–1460 cm−1 that can be assigned to C–C bending modes and the peaks in 2850–3000 cm−1 are due to the vibration modes of the C–H bond [20]. The adsorption band located around 1637 cm−1 can be attributed to the CO stretching vibration [21]. The peak of the N–H stretching mode of the CQDs can be seen at 3450 cm−1 [22]. The peak at 3465 cm−1 can also be ascribed to O–H stretching mode [23]. The characteristic absorption peaks of the drug appear around 135 cm−1, 2076 cm−1, 2333 cm−1, 2356 cm−1, and a broad peak around 3400 cm−1. The FTIR spectrum of the synthesized Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs nanocomposite shows an absorption band at 576 cm−1 which is attributed to the Fe–O stretching vibration of Fe3O4 [24]. The peaks located at 1400 cm−1 and 1608 cm−1 are assigned to the symmetric and asymmetric vibrations of COO− groups of alginate [25]. The absorption band observed in 3600 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of O–H bonds of alginate and the band around 3393 cm−1 is assigned to the bending vibrations of O–H [26]. The characteristic peak of the nanocomposite that is located at 1623 cm−1 is shifted to 1610 cm−1 for the drug-loaded nanocomposite which reveals DOX bonding on the nanocarrier.

Fig. 3 exhibits TEM images of the Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles. Fig. 3a shows that the synthesized Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles are almost monodispersed with spherical morphology. The average particle size of the Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles is below 20 nm. Comparing the mean crystallite size that has been calculated from the XRD peak broadening with the average particle size obtained by the TEM image could suggest that probably most of the particles are single crystals. Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles at a higher magnification can be seen in Fig. 3b. The nanoparticles show a core/shell morphology that confirms the perfect formation of the second phase around the bare nanoparticles.

The morphology, as well as the chemical composition (EDX) analysis of the synthesized Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs hybrid, can be seen in Fig. 4 . The FESEM images in Fig. 4a show that the nanocomposite consists of Fe3O4 nanoparticles with a mean particle size of 42 nm. The particles are agglomerated and are surrounded by a second phase that might be the alginate. The chemical composition of the nanocomposite in Fig. 4b confirms the existence of Fe, O, Si, and C. The presence of Si in the composition of the synthesized coated nanoparticles could be due to the formation of SiO2 as a coating on the nanoparticles. The TEM image of the sample (Fig. 4c) shows that by coating the nanoparticles with alginate, agglomerates of larger sizes have been formed.

Fig. 5 a shows the magnetic hysteresis loops of the Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs nanohybrid compared to the Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles. The hysteresis curves shown in Fig. 5a indicate the superparamagnetic behavior for the synthesized Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles. The saturation magnetization of the Fe3O4@SiO2 and Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs nanoparticles are 45.40 and 42.46 emu/g, respectively. The lower saturation magnetization of the nanohybrid is due to the lower fraction of magnetic nanoparticles in the unit mass of these nanoparticles. The high saturation magnetization of the nanohybrid shows that they could be potential nanocarriers in the body. The UV–Vis spectroscopy of the nanohybrid in Fig. 5b exhibits absorption in the UV range with a peak at 276 nm. Fig. 5c shows the PL spectra of the synthesized nanocomposite. The PL emission of the synthesized nanocomposite confirms the presence of the CQDs on the nanohybrid without complete quenching. The PL intensity is higher for the excitation wavelengths below 300 nm, while the nanohybrid can still show PL in higher excitation wavelengths up to 750 nm. Therefore, this nanohybrid can be used for fluorescence imaging.

Fig. 6 a shows the loading percent of the drug on the Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs nanohybrid as a function of the loading treatment duration. It can be seen that by 30 h stirring of the DOX drug with the Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs nanohybrid, a high loading percent of 79% can be achieved. Further increase in the loading process duration could not result in a considerable change in the loading efficiency. The DOX loading might have occurred by physiological interactions such as hydrophobic interactions, electrostatic, and (π-π) stacking [27]. The in vitro pH-controlled release of the Fe3O4@SiO2@al/CQDs nanohybrid was studied at pHs of 5.5 and 7.4. The time-dependent profiles of the DOX release from the nanohybrid at different pH values are illustrated in Fig. 6.

The acidic extracellular microenvironment contributes to tumor growth and metastasis. This acidic tumor microenvironment is a double-edged sword to combat cancer. The pH in tumor tissue is lower compared to normal tissues. The difference in pH value in tumors and other tissues or blood circulation can be used as an aiding agent for tumor-targeted therapy [28]. The release profile in both pH conditions in Fig. 6 shows a rapid release followed by sustained release up to 144 h. The DOX release is higher in all investigated time intervals at a pH of 5.3 compared to a pH of 7.4. The lower pH value imitates the tumor microenvironment. The DOX is released 25% within 48 h at a pH of 7.4 while is released 38% at imitated tumor environments at the same time. The slower DOX release from the synthesized nanocomposite at physiological conditions compared to fast release in simulated tumor environments makes it a suitable nanohybrid for the application as a promising drug delivery system.

In this research magneto-fluorescent Fe3O4@SiO2@alginate/CQDs nanohybrid was synthesized through the coprecipitation method. TEM images confirmed the core/shell structure of monodispersed spherical Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles with a mean particle size of around 20 nm. The Fe3O4@SiO2@alginate/CQDs nanohybrid with a saturation magnetization of 42.46 emu/g exhibited superparamagnetic behavior. The synthesized CQDs had a mean particle size below 10 nm and showed excitation wavelength-dependent PL by excitation in the range of 210–400 nm. FESEM images also showed that the nanoparticles are perfectly covered with alginate. The Fe3O4@SiO2@alginate/CQDs nanohybrid has a drug loading of 81%. The high drug (DOX) loading has been attributed to physiological interactions such as hydrophobic interactions, electrostatic, and (π-π) stacking. The Fe3O4@SiO2@alginate/CQDs nanohybrid shows a higher rate of drug release at a pH of 5.5 compared to a pH of 7.4. The magnetic and fluorescence properties, together with high drug loading and fast release in a simulated tumor environment suggest that the Fe3O4@SiO2@alginate/CQDs nanohybrid can be used as a superior multifunctional nanocarrier for drug delivery systems and bioimaging in cancer therapy.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author would like to appreciate the Shahrood University of Technology and the Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative Council for the financial support of this project.
